content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662         322-5660        322-5901        322-6441         322-6444


March 4, 1983


James C. Gross, Esq.
Dobbs & Nielsen
Suite 550, 1225 Eighth Street
Sacramento, CA  95814

                                    Re: Your Request for Written
                                        Advice, Our File No. A-83-028

Dear Mr. Gross:

    Your letter requests advice on behalf of J. Lewis Associates
and its principal, Mr. Jonathan Lewis, as well as J. Lewis
Associates' employees. The facts, as provided by you in your
letter and our several telephone conversations, are as follows:

                                FACTS

    Mr. Lewis and his firm engage in political consulting,
lobbying and Political Action Committee ("PAC") management.
Mr. Lewis and some of his employees are registered lobbyists and
lobby on behalf of various clients. J. Lewis Associates ("JLA")
has recently entered into a contract to provide consulting
services to the Block Grant Advisory Task Force created by
Section 16367 of the Government Code. This Task Force is
designed to determine the availability and recommend the
disposition of federal block grant funds made available to the
states by the Federal government under 42 U.S.C. Sections 9901,
et seq. JLA will help develop recommendations on all matters
pertinent to block grants and will provide the Task Force with
public policy development and will research information which
will be used in the Task Force's presentations to the Legislature. The federal funds will eventually be disbursed to
various local agencies and non-profit organizations that provide
social services.

    More specifically, the federal government is currently
making funds available to California and is planning to increase
that funding in the future. These funds are to be contained in
"block grants", with many fewer restrictions on their use than
have applied to "categorical" grants in the past. In order to

James C. Gross, Esq.
March 4, 1983
Page 2


determine priorities for allocation of these federal funds, the
Legislature has established the Task Force which is to gather
information as to (1) the amount of funds available; (2) what
restrictions, if any, are placed upon their use; (3) what are
the most pressing needs for these funds in California; and (4)
what are the existing mechanisms for utilization and distri-
bution of these funds, including state and local agencies and
non-profit organizations. Then the Task Force will propose
priorities for allocation of these funds to best meet the needs
as determined. A report, which includes recommendations for
legislative action and establishment of public policy, will be
forwarded by the Task Force to the Legislature as a blueprint
for a program by the state for receiving and expending the
federal block grant funds. The Block Grant Advisory Task Force
makes no final decisions regarding the disposition of funds, but
does make recommendations to the Legislature regarding fund
availability and statewide needs. The Task Force's duties are
more completely detailed in Government Code Section 16367(d),
enacted by Chap. 1343, Stats. 1982, a copy of which is attached
as Exhibit "A".

Under Government Code Section 16367.2(c) $135,000 is
appropriated by the Legislature from the Federal Block Grant
Fund to the Controller's office for disbursement to pay for
administrative costs of the Task Force. Those are the funds to
be paid to JLA in its conduct of the administrative activities
of the Task Force. In effect, JLA's employees will be function-
ing as the staff for the Task Force.

According to the contract between JLA and the Block Grant
Advisory Task Force, JLA is to serve in a policy-making capacity
and as a liaison between the Task Force and the Legislature.
However, both JLA and the Task Force have agreed that JLA and
its employees and representatives shall perform administrative
and consulting tasks and no lobbying activities. (See attached
agreement).

Employees of JLA lobby for a number of organizations. One
of those organizations is the Jewish Public Affairs Committee
("JPAC"). JPAC is not eligible for any Block Grant funds.
JPAC's only interest is in seeing that the funds are equitably
disbursed according to each community's needs. JLA's
relationship with JPAC is in no way connected to or contingent
upon its relationship with the Block Grant Advisory Task Force,
and JLA has no intention of lobbying the Block Grant Advisory
Task Force on behalf of JPAC.

James C. Gross, Esq.
March 4, 1983
Page 3


     JLA has disclosed its relationship with JPAC to all members
of the Task Force and has disclosed its relationship with the
Task Force to JPAC. It has advised both parties that it will
disqualify itself in any decisions that affect JPAC or any of
its members.

                    QUESTIONS AND ANALYSES

     You have posed several questions based upon the foregoing
facts which will be restated and answered in turn.

Question 1:  Is the Block Grant Advisory Task Force a "State
Agency" under the Political Reform Act?

Analysis

     The Task Force is a state agency. It is involved in
performing the public's work, using public funds. Its members
are all appointees of the Governor or the Legislature and
include the Director of Finance, the Chair of the Senate Finance
Committee and the Chair of the Assembly Ways and Means
Committee. It is officially sanctioned to produce a work
product on which the Legislature and the Governor will place
heavy reliance.

     In your letter requesting advice, you have directed our
attention to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18249, which defines
whether a public entity is state or local for purposes of the
lobbying registration and reporting requirements of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act")1/.  (See, Opinion requested
by James J. Vonk, 6 FPPC Opinions 4 at 8, No. 80-008, March 2,
1981.) Because there are some lobbying questions involved here,
we shall consider its applicability. Regulation 18249 provides
as follows:

     An agency is a state agency within the provisions
of Government Code Sections 82004 and 86100-86300 only
if all the following criteria are met:

          (a) The agency is authorized by statute,
executive order or the California Constitution.

_________________________

1/  Government Code Sections 81000-91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code unless
otherwise specified.

James C. Gross, Esq.
March 4, 1983
Page 4


(b)  At least one voting member is an elected
state officer or is appointed by an elected state
officer or an agency official or a state agency.

(c)  The agency is financed in part by any
state funds or is subject to appropriation in the
state budget.

(d)  An area larger than one county is
included in its jurisdiction.

The Task Force is created by statute and its composition is
set out in that statute.  Section 16367.  Clearly, the Task
Force meets the criteria of Regulation 18249(a) and (b).  Just
as clearly, its jurisdiction is statewide, meeting the criteria
of subdivision (d).

In your letter, you have urged that the method of funding of
the Task Force by subsection (c) is not encompassed by subdivision (e) in that the
ultimate funding source is the Federal Government and that the
funds were not specifically appropriated in the state budget.
We respectfully disagree.  In the Vonk Opinion, supra at p.8, we
stated, in footnote 3:

The Commission's deliberations concerning
subsection (c) indicate that it was intended to cover
any funds which flow through the State Treasury and any
funds which are raised pursuant to state authority,
such as agricultural and industry assessments, in
addition to regular state appropriations.

Section 16367.2, which provides the funding for the Task
Force, reads as follows:

(a)  There is hereby created in the State Treasury
the Federal Block Grant Fund, to which there is
allocated one hundred thirty-five thousand dollars
($135,000) from those federal block grant funds
designated by the Budget Act of 1982 for block grant
administrative costs incurred by administering state
agencies.

(b)  The appropriation by the Budget Act of 1982
to each administering state agency for federal block
grant administrative costs shall be reduced by a
proportionate share for allocation to the Federal Block

James C. Gross, Esq.
March 4, 1983
Page 5


     Grant Fund pursuant to subdivision (a), with the
exception of the Education and Community Development
block grants.

     (c) The sum of one hundred thirty-five thousand
dollars ($135,000) is hereby appropriated from the
Federal Block Grant Fund to the Controller for
disbursement in support of the clock grant advisory
task force under Section 16367 during the 1982-83
fiscal year.

     It is the intent of the Legislature that there
exist an operating expense budget to cover the
operational costs of the block grant advisory task
force required pursuant to Article 1.7 (commencing with
Section 16366.1). These costs shall include, but are
not limited to, staffing, operating expenses, per diem,
public notice, and printing costs.

     (d) This section shall remain in effect only
until July 1, 1984, and as of that date is repealed.

     A careful reading of the statutory provision makes it clear
that these are funds which flow through the State Treasury. The
sum of $135,000 is appropriated by the Legislature and an
expense budget is established. The fact that the appropriation
"sunsets" after one and one-half years does not alter the nature
of the funding. It is always the case that when a new state
agency or program will become operative on January First, 2/
the legislation creating it must contain an appropriation
because the agency will not be included in the Budget Act for
the current fiscal year. This does not mean that such an agency
is not a "state agency" within the meaning of the Act. Further-
more, as specifically set forth in Section 16367.2(b), the funds
here are being reallocated from appropriations contained in the
Budget Act of 1982 and, consequently, Chap. 1343 acts as an
amendment to the Budget Act of 1982.

Conclusion

     Because of its duties and status, the Task Force is a state
agency within the meaning of the Act generally. We further
conclude that the Block Grant Advisory Task Force is a state,

___________________

     2/ The appropriation contained in Section 16367.2 was
enacted in Chap. 1343, Stats. 1982, and became effective
January 1, 1983.

James C. Gross, Esq.
March 4, 1983
Page 6


rather than a local, agency within the meaning of the lobbying
provisions of the Act.

Question 2:

    Are the members of the Task Force and the employees of
J. Lewis Associates who are working for the Task Force "public
officials" within the meaning of the Political Reform Act?

Analysis

    "Public official" as defined in Section 82048, includes
every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or
local government agency. Since the Task Force is a state
agency, members of the Task Force are public officials under
Section 82004. Employees of JLA who work for the Task Force are
either employees or consultants (as defined in 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18700(a)(2)) of a state agency and hence "public
officials" under Section 82048.

Question 3:

    Are J. Lewis Associates' employees who are working on the
Task Force project subject to the Conflict of Interest
Provisions of the Political Reform Act?

Analysis

    Section 87100 provides that:

        No public official at any level of state or local
    government shall make, participate in making or in any
    way attempt to use his official position to influence a
    governmental decision in which he knows or has reason
    to know he has a financial interest.

                                    (Emphasis added.)

Consequently, as public officials the members and staff of the
Task Force are covered by the Conflict of Interest provisions of
the Act requiring disqualification where appropriate.

Question 4:

    Are the Task Force's members and staff covered by the
Legislature's Conflict of Interest Code?

James C. Gross, Esq.
March 4, 1983
Page 7


Analysis

As you have pointed out in your letter, the Legislature's
Code will cover the Task Force (under Section 3). If the Task
Force is considered to be a "joint committee." If that is the
case, then the non-clerical staff and the members of the Task
Force not already covered by Article 2 of Chapter Seven of the
Act would be designated employees under that section. However,
this would be so only if it is determined that the Task Force is
a Joint Committee. That is a question which should be addressed
to the Legislature or perhaps Legislative Counsel. If the Task
Force is not covered by the Legislature's Conflict of Interest
Code, then it should adopt its own Code with a list of desig-
nated employees. We are prepared to assist in expediting this
process. However, you should remember that the disqualification
provisions of Section 87100 apply whether or not the Task Force
is currently covered by a Code.

Question 5:

What conflict of interest issues are raised under Chapter 7
of the Act by JLA's relationship with JPAC and the Task Force?

Analysis

In order for there to be a conflict of interest under
Chapter 7 of the Act (Sections 87100, et seq.) requiring
disqualification by an official, there must first be a financial
interest. Section 87103 defines when an official has a
financial interest. The pertinent portion is set forth below.

An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on:

* * *

(c) Any source of income, other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the regular
course of business on terms available to the
public without regard to official status, aggre-
gating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in
value provided to, received by or promised to the
public official within 12 months prior to the time
when the decision is made.

James C. Gross, Esq.
March 4, 1983
Page 8


                    (d)  Any business entity in which the public
           official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
           employee, or holds any position of management.

      The Jewish Public Affairs Council is a source of income to
J. Lewis Associates. However, it may or may not be a source of
income to individual employees of JLA. Almost assuredly it is a
source of income to Mr. Lewis; we assume he owns more than 10%
of JLA. Pursuant to Sections 82030(a) and 87103(c), if his pro
rata share of JLA's gross receipts from JPAC was $250 or more
during the preceding 12-month period, then he would have a
financial interest in JPAC, which could lead to disqualification.

      We have no information as to other employees to determine
whether JPAC is a source of income to them. However, if an
employee owns a 10% or greater share of JLA, then JPAC may be a
source of income to that employee under Sections 82030(a) and
87103(c). Moreover, even if JPAC is not a source of income, JLA
is a source of income for each of the employees, as well as for
Mr. Lewis. Thus, if a decision affected JPAC to such an extent
that it resulted in a material financial effect on JLA, dis-
qualification would be required. Given the fact that JPAC is
not eligible for Block Grant funds, and that JLA's relationship
with JPAC "is in no way connected to or contingent upon" JLA's
relationship with the Task Force, it is not reasonably foresee-
able that any Task Force decision will have a material financial
effect (as defined in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(1)) on
JPAC, or on JLA as a result of its relationship to JPAC. In any
event, JLA and its associates voluntarily have agreed to dis-
qualify themselves from any decision which could affect JPAC.

      However, for Mr. Lewis (and any other 10% owner of JLA), we
must also consider the provision in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18702(b)(3)(B) which provides that if there is a "nexus" between
the purpose for which he receives income from JPAC and the
governmental decisions he makes for the Task Force, then there
is a material financial effect. He will be required to
disqualify himself whenever such a "nexus" is present and the
effect on JPAC is distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally.3/ Mr. Lewis has already agreed to do this. For
_________________________

      3/ See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703 for a discussion of
the application of the "public generally" concept. Here the
jurisdiction is the entire State of California.

James C. Gross, Esq.
March 4, 1983
Page 9


further discussion of the "nexus" principle, please see the
enclosed advice letters to Jeffrey Gunther (No. A-82-055) and
Linda Best (No. A-81-032).

Conclusion

     There does not appear to be any likelihood of conflict of
interest disqualification for JLA employees based upon the fact
that JPAC is a lobbying client of JLA. However, Mr. Lewis (and
any other 10% owner of JLA) may have a conflict requiring
disqualification as to any decisions meeting the "nexus" test
with respect to JPAC.

Question 6:

     Must JLA report the Task Force as a lobbying client?

Analysis

     Since the Task Force is a state agency within the meaning of
Regulation 18249, it follows that Section 86300(a) applies
here. This section excludes persons lobbying for state agencies
from most of the requirements and restrictions placed on other
lobbyists, specifically exempting from registration and
reporting. Consequently, neither JLA nor its lobbyist employees
need to register the Task Force as a lobbying client.

Conclusion

     J. Lewis Associates need not file an authorization from the
Task Force to lobby on its behalf and need not report the Task
Force as a client.

     We trust that this letter has adequately responded to the
questions raised by you. Should you have further questions
regarding this matter, you may contact me at 916/322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Robert R. Leidigh
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

REL:km

DOBBS & NIELSEN
Attorneys and Counsellors at Law

SAN FRANCISCO OFFICE
SUITE 2900, THE RUSS BUILDING
ONE MARITIME PLAZA
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94111
(415) 362-1940

A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING PROFESSIONAL CORPORATIONS

SUITE 550, 1225 EIGHTH STREET
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814

LOS ANGELES OFFICE
SUITE 309, 648 SOUTH FLOWER STREET
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90017
(213) 628-5867

(916) 446-6752

NEWPORT BEACH OFFICE
SUITE 4000, 4000 MACARTHUR BOULEVARD
NEWPORT BEACH, CALIFORNIA 92660
(714) 752-9318

February 1, 1983


Barbara Milman
Director, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Ms. Milman:

     This office is writing on behalf of Jonathan Lewis and
his consulting firm J. Lewis Associates. Mr. Lewis and his firm
engage in political consulting, lobbying and PAC management. Mr.
Lewis and some of his employees are registered lobbyists and
lobby on behalf of various clients.

     J. Lewis Associates has recently entered into a con-
tract to provide consulting services to the Block Grant Advisory
Task Force created by §16367 of the Government Code. This task
force is designed to determine the availability and recommend the
disposition of federal block grant funds made available to the
states by the federal government under 42 U.S.C. §§9901 et seq.
J. Lewis Associates will help develop recommendations on all
matters pertinent to block grants and will provide the task force
with public policy development and research information which
will be used in the task force's presentations to the
legislature. The federal funds will eventually be disbursed to
various local agencies and non-profit organizations that provide
social services.

     As mentioned previously, employees of J. Lewis Associ-
ates lobby for a number of organizations. One of those organiza-
tions is the Jewish Public Affairs Committee. The Jewish Public
Affairs Committee has a general concern regarding how the Block
Grant funds will be disbursed. It is for this reason that J.
Lewis Associates is concerned with its responsibilities under the
conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act. The
four questions posed below relate to the applicability of the
conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act to J.
Lewis Associates and its employees and whether the Block Grant
Advisory Task Force is a lobbying client of J. Lewis Associates.

     Question No. 1: Is J. Lewis Associates or any of its
employees subject to the Conflict of Interest Code of the State
Legislature?

It is our understanding that only designated employees
of the Legislature must comply with the Conflict of Interest Code
of the State Legislature. In order for J. Lewis Associates to be
subject to this code, the block grant task force must be con-
sidered a "Legislative Committee." Sections 16367 et seq. of the
Government Code, dealing with the creation and operation of the
task force, contains no language indicating that the task force
is a Legislative Committee. Nor is the task force funded by any
state funds. Further, the task force is of only a one year
duration. Finally, only two legislators sit on this task force.

With the foregoing information in mind, we would like
your opinion whether J. Lewis Associates must comply with the
provisions of the Conflict of Interest Code of the State Legisla-
ture because of its relationship with the Block Grant Advisory
Task Force.

Question No. 2: Is J. Lewis Associates or any of its
employees "Public Officials" and thus subject to the Conflict of
Interest provisions under §87100 et seq. of the Political Reform
Act?

Under §87100 of the Political Reform Act, "no public
official at any level of state or local government shall make,
participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official
position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows
or has reason to know he has a financial interest." "Public
official" is defined in §82048 as "every member, officer,
employee or consultant of a state or local government agency."
Under §18249 of the FPPC regulations an organization is only a
state agency if the four stated criteria are met. According to
subsection (c), an entity is not a state agency if it is not
financed in part by some state funds or subject to appropriation
in the state budget. There is some doubt as to whether
subsection (c) of §18249 is satisfied.

Under Government Code §16367.2(c) $135,000 is appro-
priated from the Federal Block Grant Fund to pay for administra-
tive costs of the task force. Those are the funds to be paid to
J. Lewis Associates in its carrying out of the administrative
functions of the task force. According to §16367.2(c) those
funds are appropriated to the controller's office for disburse-
ment in support of the task force. Thus, the funds appropriated
are federal rather than state and the appropriation is to the
state controller rather than the task force. The question is
whether this disbursement subjects the task force to "appropria-
tion in the state budget" and thus makes the task force a
state agency.

Question No. 3: If the Block Grant Advisory Task Force
is a "State Agency" and J. Lewis Associates et al. are "Public
Officials," must they disqualify themselves from involvement with
the Task Force under §§87100 through 87103 of the Political
Reform Act?

Section 87103 states that one has a "financial
interest" if it is "reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect distinguishable from its effect
on the public generally." Thus, the question is whether J. Lewis
Associates' work on the task force will have a material financial
effect on its relationship with the Jewish Public Affairs Committee.

It is important to note that the Block Grant Advisory
Task Force makes no decisions regarding the disposition of funds
but only makes recommendations to the legislature regarding fund
availability and statewide needs. Also, the Jewish Public
Affairs Committee is not eligible for any Block Grant funds.
Rather, only community-based organizations are considered for
Block Grant funds. The Jewish Public Affairs committee's only
interest is in seeing that the funds are equitably disbursed
according to each community's needs. Additionally, we wish to
point out that J. Lewis Associates' relationship with the Jewish
Public Affairs Committee is in no way connected to or contingent
upon its relationship with the Block Grant Advisory Task Force.
Finally, J. Lewis Associates has no intention of lobbying the
Block Grant Advisory Task Force on behalf of the Jewish Public
Affairs Committee.

J. Lewis Associates has disclosed its relationship with
the Jewish Public Affairs Committee to all members of the task
force and has disclosed its relationship with the task force to
the Jewish Public Affairs Committee. It has advised both parties
that it will disqualify itself in any decisions that affect the
Jewish Public Affairs Committee or any of its members.

It is with the above information in mind that J. Lewis
Associates requests an opinion from the FPPC as to whether it has
a material financial interest in the decision of the task force
and if so, if its proposal to disqualify itself in limited situations avoids a violation of §§87100 - 87103.

Question No. 4: Must J. Lewis Associates register the
Block Grant Advisory Task Force as a lobbying client?

According to the contract between J. Lewis Associates
and the Block Grant Advisory Task Force, J. Lewis Associates is
to serve in a policy-making capacity and as a liaison between the
task force and the Legislature. No where in the contract does it
state that J. Lewis Associates will lobby for the task force. In
fact, both J. Lewis Associates and the task force have agreed
that J. Lewis Associates and its employees and representatives
shall perform administrative and consulting tasks and no lobbying
activities. (See attached agreement). Nonetheless, because J.
Lewis Associates is registered as a lobbyist for other
organizations and because it will have contact with members and
employees of the Legislature it is concerned as to whether the
task force must be registered as a lobbying client.

We would appreciate a prompt reply to the questions
stated due to the time constraints involved. If you have any
questions please feel free to call.

Sincerely,

[signature]

JAMES C. GROSS

JCG:ss

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660          322-5901         322-6641              322-6444

February 8, 1983


James C. Gross
Dobbs & Nielsen
1225 Eighth Street, Suite 550
Sacramento, CA 95814

                                        Re: A-83-028

Dear Mr. Gross:

     Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to Robert E. Leidigh, an
attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political
Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact this attorney directly at
(916) 322-5901.

     We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

                                        Very truly yours,


                                        Barbara A. Milman
                                        Chief
                                        Legal Division

BAM:km
Enclosure

DOBBS & NIELSEN
Attorneys and Counselors at Law

SAN FRANCISCO OFFICE
SUITE 2500, THE ALCOA BUILDING
ONE MARKET STREET
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94111
(415) 362-1934

LOS ANGELES OFFICE
SUITE 309, 612 SOUTH FLOWER STREET
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90017
(213) 623-8384

A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING PROFESSIONAL CORPORATIONS
SUITE 550, 1275 EIGHTH STREET
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 446-6752

NEWPORT BEACH OFFICE
SUITE 4000, 4000 MACARTHUR BOULEVARD
NEWPORT BEACH, CALIFORNIA 92660
(714) 752-9318

February 1, 1983

Barbara Milman
Director, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Ms. Milman:

This office is writing on behalf of Jonathan Lewis and
his consulting firm J. Lewis Associates. Mr. Lewis and his firm
engage in political consulting, lobbying and PAC management. Mr.
Lewis and some of his employees are registered lobbyists and
lobby on behalf of various clients.

J. Lewis Associates has recently entered into a contract to provide consulting services to the Block Grant Advisory
Task Force created by §16367 of the Government Code. This task
force is designed to determine the availability and recommend the
disposition of federal block grant funds made available to the
states by the federal government under 42 U.S.C. §§9901 et seq.
J. Lewis Associates will help develop recommendations on all
matters pertinent to block grants and will provide the task force
with public policy development and research information which
will be used in the task force's presentations to the
legislature. The federal funds will eventually be disbursed to
various local agencies and non-profit organizations that provide
social services.

As mentioned previously, employees of J. Lewis Associates lobby for a number of organizations. One of those organizations is the Jewish Public Affairs Committee. The Jewish Public
Affairs Committee has a general concern regarding how the Block
Grant funds will be disbursed. It is for this reason that J.
Lewis Associates is concerned with its responsibilities under the
conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act. The
four questions posed below relate to the applicability of the
conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act to J.
Lewis Associates and its employees and whether the Block Grant
Advisory Task Force is a lobbying client of J. Lewis Associates.

Question No. 1: Is J. Lewis Associates or any of its
employees subject to the Conflict of Interest Code of the State
Legislature?

Not dependent on "new federalism"

It is our understanding that only designated employees
of the Legislature must comply with the Conflict of Interest Code
of the State Legislature. In order for J. Lewis Associates to be
subject to this code, the block grant task force must be con-
sidered a "Legislative Committee." Sections 16367 et seq. of the
Government Code, dealing with the creation and operation of the
task force, contains no language indicating that the task force
is a Legislative Committee. Nor is the task force funded by any
state funds. Further, the task force is of only a one year
duration. Finally, only two legislators sit on this task Force.

With the foregoing information in mind, we would like
your opinion whether J. Lewis Associates must comply with the
provisions of the Conflict of Interest Code of the State Legisla-
ture because of its relationship with the Block Grant Advisory
Task Force.

Question No. 2: Is J. Lewis Associates or any of its
employees "Public Officials" and thus subject to the Conflict of
Interest provisions under §87100 et seq. of the Political Reform
Act?

Under §87100 of the Political Reform Act, "no public
official at any level of state or local government shall make,
participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official
position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows
or has reason to know he has a financial interest." "Public
official" is defined in §82048 as "every member, officer,
employee or consultant of a state or local government agency."
Under §18249 of the PRC regulations an organization is only a state
agency if the four stated criteria are met. According to
subsection (c), an entity is not a state agency if it is not
financed in part by some state funds or subject to appropriation
in the state budget. There is some doubt as to whether
subsection (c) of §18249 is satisfied.

Under Government Code §16367.2(c) $135,000 is appro-
priated from the Federal Block Grant Fund to pay for administra-
tive costs of the task force. Those are the funds to be paid to
J. Lewis Associates in its carrying out of the administrative
functions of the task force. According to §16367.2(c) those
funds are appropriated to the controller's office for disburse-
ment in support of the task force. Thus, the funds appropriated
are federal rather than state and the appropriation is to the
state controller rather than the task force. The question is
whether this disbursement subjects the task force to "appropria-
tion in the state budget" and thus makes the task force a
state agency.

Question No. 3: If the Block Grant Advisory Task Force
is a "State Agency" and J. Lewis Associates et al. are "Public
Officials," must they disqualify themselves from involvement with
the Task Force under §§87100 through 87103 of the Political
Reform Act?

Section 87103 states that one has a "financial
interest" if it is "reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect distinguishable from its effect
on the public generally." Thus, the question is whether J. Lewis
Associates' work on the task force will have a material financial
effect on its relationship with the Jewish Public Affairs Committee.

It is important to note that the Block Grant Advisory
Task Force makes no decisions regarding the disposition of funds
but only makes recommendations to the legislature regarding fund
availability and statewide needs. Also, the Jewish Public
Affairs Committee is not eligible for any Block Grant funds.
Rather, only community-based organizations are considered for
Block Grant funds. The Jewish Public Affairs Committee's only
interest is in seeing that the funds are equitably disbursed
according to each community's needs. Additionally, we wish to
point out that J. Lewis Associates' relationship with the Jewish
Public Affairs Committee is in no way connected to or contingent
upon its relationship with the Block Grant Advisory Task Force.
Finally, J. Lewis Associates has no intention of lobbying the
Block Grant Advisory Task Force on behalf of the Jewish Public
Affairs Committee.

J. Lewis Associates has disclosed its relationship with
the Jewish Public Affairs Committee to all members of the task
force and has disclosed its relationship with the task force to
the Jewish Public Affairs Committee. It has advised both parties
that it will disqualify itself in any decisions that affect the
Jewish Public Affairs Committee or any of its members.

It is with the above information in mind that J. Lewis
Associates requests an opinion from the FPPC as to whether it has
a material financial interest in the decision of the task force
and if so, if its proposal to disqualify itself in limited situations avoids a violation of §§87100 - 87103.

Question No. 4: Must J. Lewis Associates register the
Block Grant Advisory Task Force as a lobbying client?

According to the contract between J. Lewis Associates
and the Block Grant Advisory Task Force, J. Lewis Associates is
to serve in a policy-making capacity and as a liaison between the
task force and the Legislature. No where in the contract does it
state that J. Lewis Associates will lobby for the task force. In
fact, both J. Lewis Associates and the task force have agreed
that J. Lewis Associates and its employees and representatives
shall perform administrative and consulting tasks and no lobbying
activities. (See attached agreement). Nonetheless, because J.
Lewis Associates is registered as a lobbyist for other
organizations and because it will have contact with members and
employees of the Legislature it is concerned as to whether the
task force must be registered as a lobbying client.

We would appreciate a prompt reply to the questions
stated due to the time constraints involved. If you have any
questions please feel free to call.

Sincerely,

[signature]

JAMES C. GROSS

JCG:ss

Ch. 1343                STATUTES OF 1982

FISCAL AFFAIRS—FEDERAL BLOCK GRANTS
Assembly Bill No. 3295

CHAPTER 1343

An act to amend Section 16367 of, to add and repeal Section 16367.2
of, and to add Article 7 (commencing with Section 53120) to Chapter
1 of Part 1 of Division 2 of Title 5 of, and to repeal and add Section
16367.7 to, the Government Code, relating to fiscal affairs.

[Approved by Governor September 23, 1982. Filed with
Secretary of State September 24, 1982.]

LEGISLATIVE COUNSEL'S DIGEST

AB 3295, Agnos. Fiscal affairs; block grants.
Existing law provides for the receipt and distribution of federal
block grants for the 1981-82 fiscal year.
This bill would set forth findings and declarations of the
legislature with respect to federal block grants, would specify the
federal block grants to be assumed in the 1982-83 fiscal year and the
services to be provided, and would specify the procedures applicable
to the administration of federal block grants for the 1982-83 fiscal
year.
Existing law establishes a block grant advisory task force.
This bill would revise the duties of the task force, as specified.
In addition, the bill would create in the State Treasury the Federal
Block Grant Fund to which there is allocated $135,000 from federal
block grant funds designated by the Budget Act of 1982 to be
appropriated, as specified.

The people of the State of California do enact as follows:

SECTION 1. The Legislature hereby finds and declares all of the
following:

(a) The federal government has enacted and is proposing
significant changes in the structure and funding of social services by
eliminating or greatly reducing many categorical grants and
consolidating them into block grants to be administered by the state.

(b) Interim legislation for the receipt and distribution of federal
block grants for the 1981-82 fiscal year was enacted by Chapter 1186
of the Statutes of 1981.

(c) Additional legislation for the 1982-83 fiscal year is necessary
to aid in the continuing transition in the structure and funding of social
services.

(d) This legislation must preserve quality services whenever
possible, despite reductions in federal funding and economic
downturns. Even though there may be drastic reductions in funds,
there are not commensurate reductions in persons' needs.

7192                Changes or additions in text are indicated by underline

1981-1982 REGULAR SESSION                Ch. 1343

(e) When reductions in service must be made, there must be an
orderly, protective transition process which recognizes human
dignity among both the service population and the workers who
serve them. Both the service population and the workers must have
a substantial role in targeting reductions and deciding how
reductions will be implemented.
(f) It is necessary that the mechanism for the delivery of services
be brought as close as possible to the people being served, provided
that these services satisfy state standards and basic protections are
observed.
(g) Critical data regarding numerous block grant programs does
not exist at the present time. This information is essential before
a major redistribution of block grant funds can be done on an informed
basis.
SEC. 2. The Legislature directs the state to assume
administrative responsibility for the following federal block grants in
the 1982-83 state fiscal year:
(a) Preventative health and health services, effective July 1, 1982.
(b) Maternal and child health, effective July 1, 1982.
(c) Alcohol, drug abuse and mental health services, effective July
1, 1982.
(d) Community services, effective October 1, 1982.
(e) Community development block grant, effective October 1,
1982.
SEC. 3. Due to substantial state matching requirements, the
Legislature directs the state to not assume the primary care block
grant.
SEC. 4. To the extent it is not in conflict with the actions taken
in the Budget Act of 1982, Section 16366.6 of the Government Code
shall remain in effect during the 1982-83 fiscal year or until separate
legislation is enacted.
SEC. 5. The assignment of administrative responsibilities to
specific state departments shall be determined as part of the annual
budget process.
SEC. 6. Section 16366.7 of the Government Code is repealed.
SEC. 7. Section 16366.7 is added to the Government Code, to
read:
16366.7. Since federal block grant funds were reduced by an
average of 26 percent during the 1981-82 fiscal year and are proposed
for further reductions during the 1982-83 fiscal year, the Legislature
declares that the state's administrative costs and processes must be
reduced in order to insure that maximum funds are available to
continue essential direct human services.
Therefore, notwithstanding any other provision of law, all of the
following state procedures shall be implemented within 60 days after
the effective date of this section:
(a) All state agencies, offices, or departments administering
federal block grant funds shall have the authority, subject to the
approval of the Department of Finance, to grant advance payments

sections.question:
Is the Block Grant Advisory Task Force a "State 
Agency" under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion:
Because of its duties and status, the Task Force is a state 
agency within the meaning of the Act generally. 
We further 
conclude that the Block Grant Advisory Task Force is a state, 
~I The appropriation contained in Section 16367.2 was 
enacted in Chap. 1343, Stats. 1982, and became effective 
January 1, 1983. 


James C. Gross, Esq. 
March 4,1983  
rather than a local, agency within the meaning of tne lobbying 
provisions of the Act. 
Question 2: 
Are the members of the Task Force and the employees of 
J. Lewis Associates who are working for the Task Force "public 
officials" within the meaning of the Political Reform Act? 
Analysis 
"Public official" as defined in Section 82048, includes 
every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or 
local government agency. 
Since the Task Force is a 
stat~ 
agency, members of the Task Force are public officials under 
Section 82004. 
Employees of JLA who work for the Task Force are 
either employees or consultants (as defined in 2 Cal. Adm. Code 
Section 18700 (a) (2) 
0 f a state agency and hence "public 
officials" under Section 82048. 
Question 3: 
Are J. Lewis Associates' employees who are working on the 
Task Force project subject to the Conflict of Interest 
Provisions of the Political Reform Act? 
Analysis 
Section 87100 provides that: 
No public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any 
way attempt to use his official position to influence a 
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason 
to know he has a financial interest. 
(Emphasis added.) 
Consequently, as public officials the members and staff of the 
Task Force are covered by the Conflict of Interest provisions of 
the Act requiring disqualification where appropriate. 
Question 4: 
Are the Task Force's members and staff covered by the 
Legislature's Conflict of Interest Code? 


James C. Gross, Esq. 
March 4, 1983  
Analysis 
As you have pointed out in your letter, the Legislature's 
Code will cover the Task Force (under Section 3), if the Task 
Force is considered to be a "joint committee." 
If that is the 
case, then the non-clerical staff and the members of the Task 
Force not already covered by Article 2 of Chapter Seven of the 
Act would be designated employees under that section. 
However, 
this would be so only if it is determined that the Task Force is 
a Joint Committee. 
That is a question which should be addressed 
to the Legislature or perhaps Legislative Counsel. 
If the Task 
Force is not covered by the Legislature's Conflict of Interest 
Code, then it should adopt its own Code with a list of desig-
nated employees. 
We are prepared to assist in expediting this 
process. 
However, you should remember that the disqualification 
provisions of Section 87100 apply whether or not the Task Force 
is currently covered by a Code. 
Question 5: 
What conflict of interest issues are raised under Chapter 7 
of the Act by JLA's relationship with JPAC and the Task Force? 
Analysis 
In order for there to be a conflict of interest under 
Chapter 7 of the Act (Sections 87100, et seq.) requiring 
disqualification by an official, there-mu~first be a financial 
interest. 
Section 87103 defines when an official has a 
financial interest. 
The pertinent portion is set forth below. 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally, on:
sections.facts:
Mr. Lewis and his firm engage in political consulting, 
lobbying and Political Action Committee ("PAC") management. 
Mr. Lewis and some of his employees are registered lobbyists and 
lobby on behalf of various clients. 
J. Lewis Associates ("JLA") 
has recently entered into a contract to provide consulting 
services to the Block Grant Advisory Task Force created by 
Section 16367 of the Government Code. 
This Task Force is 
designed to determine the availability and recommend the 
disposition of federal block grant funds made available to the 
states by the federal government under 42 U.S.C. Sections 9901, 
et~. JLA will help develop recommendations on all matters 
pertinent to block grants and will provide the Task Force with 
public policy development and will research information which 
will be used in the Task Force's presentations to the Legis-
lature. 
The federal funds will eventually be disbursed to 
various local agencies and non-profit organizations that provide 
social services. 
More specifically, the federal government is currently 
making funds available to California and is planning to increase 
that funding in the future. 
These funds are to b~ contained in 
"block grants", with many fewer restrictions on their use 
have applied to "categorical" grants in the past. 
In order to 


James C. Gross, Esq. 
March 4, 1983  
determine priorities for allocation of these federal funds, the 
Legislature has established the Task Force which is to gather 
information as to (1) the amount of funds available; (2) what 
restrictions, if any, are placed upon their use; (3) what are 
the most pressing needs for these funds in California; and (4) 
what are the existing mechanisms for utilization and distri-
bution of these funds, including state and local agencies and 
non-profit organizations. 
Then the Task Force will propose 
priorities for allocation of these funds to best meet the needs 
as determined. 
A report, which includes recommendations for 
legislative action and establishment of public policy, will be 
forwarded by the Task Force to the Legislature as a blueprint 
for a program by the state for receiving and expending the 
federal block grant funds. 
The Block Grant Advisory Task Force 
makes no final decisions regarding the disposition of funds, but 
does make recommendations to the Legislature regarding fund 
availability and statewide needs. 
The Task Force's duties are 
more completely detailed in Government Code Section 16367(d) I 
enacted by Chap. 1343, Stats. 1982, a copy of which is attached 
a s Exh i bit " A " • 
.. 
Under Government Code Section 16367.2(c) $135,000 is 
appropriated by the Legislature from the Federal Block Grant 
Fund to the Controller's office for disbursement to pay for 
administrative costs of the Task Force. 
Those are the funds to 
be paid to JLA in its conduct of the administrative activities 
of the Task Force. 
In effect, JLA's employees will be function-
ing as the staff for the Task Force. 
According to the contract between JLA and the Block Grant 
Advisory Task Force, JLA is to serve in a policy-making capacity 
and as a liaison between the Task Force and the Legislature. 
However, both JLA and the Task Force have agreed that JLA and 
its employees and representatives shall perform administrative 
and consulting tasks and no lobbying activities. 
(See attached 
agreement) . 
Employees of JLA lobby for a number of organizations. 
One 
of those organizations is the Jewish public Affairs Committee 
("JPAC"). 
JPAC is not eligible for any Block Grant funds. 
JPAC's only interest is in seeing that tne funds are equitably 
disbursed according to each community's needs. 
JLA's 
relationship with JPAC is in no way connected to or contingent 
upon its relationship with the Block Grant Advisory Task Force, 
and JLA has no intention of lobbying the Block Grant Advisory 
Task Force on behalf of JPAC. 


James C. Gross, Esq. 
March 4, 1983  
JLA has disclosed its relationship with JPAC to all members 
of the Task Force and has disclosed its relationship with the 
Task Force to JPAC. 
It has advised both parties that it will 
disqualify itself in any decisions that affect JPAC or any of 
its members. 
QUESTIONS AND ANALYSES 
You have posed several questions based upon the foregoing 
facts which will be restated and answered in turn.
sections.analysis:
The Task Force is a state agency. 
It is involved in 
performing the public's work, using public funds. 
Its members 
are all appointees of the Governor or the Legislature and 
include the Director of Finance, the Chair of the Senate Finance 
Committee and the Chair of the Assembly Ways and Means 
Committee. 
It is officially sanctioned to produce a work 
product on which the Legislature and the Governor will place 
heavy reliance. 
In your letter requesting advice, you have directed our 
attention to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18249, which defines 
whether a public entity .is state or local for purposes of the 
lobbying registration and reporting requirements of the 
political Reform Act (the !tAct")l/. 
(See, Opinion requested 
by James J. Vonk, 6 FPPC Opinions 4 at-S; No. 80-008, March 2, 
1981.) 
Because there are some lobbying questions involved here, 
we shall consider its applicability. 
Regulation 18249 provides 
as follows: 
An agency is a state agency within the provisions 
of Government Code Sections 82004 and 86100-86300 only 
if all the following criteria are met: 
(a) 
The agency is authorized by statute, 
executive order or the California Constitution. 
!/ Government Code Sections 81000-91014. 
All 
statutory references are to the Government Code unless 
otherwise specif~ed. 


James C. Gross, Esq. 
March 4, 1983  
(b) 
At least one voting member is an elected 
state officer or is appointed by an elected state 
officer or an agency official or a state agency. 
(c) 
The agency is financed in part by any 
state funds or is subject to appropriation in the 
state budget. 
(d) 
An area larger than one county is 
included in its jurisdiction. 
The Task Force is created by statute and its composition is 
set out in that statute. 
Section 16367. 
Clearly, the Task 
Force meets the criteria of Regulation l8249(a) and (b). 
Just 
as clearly, its jurisdiction is statewide, meeting the criteria 
of subdivision (d). 
In your letter, you have urged that the method of funding of 
the Task Force is not encompassed by subdivision (c) in that the 
ultimate funding source is the Federal Government and tbat the 
funds were not specifically appropriated in the state budget. 
We respectfully disagree. 
In the Vonk Opinion, supra at p.8, we 
stated, in footnote 3: 
The Commission's deliberations concerning 
subsection (c) indicate that it was intended to cover 
any funds which flow through the State Treasury and any 
funds which are raised pursuant to state authority, 
such as agricultural and industry assessments, in 
addition to regular state appropriations. 
Section 16367.2, which provides the funding for the Task 
Force, reads as follows: 
(a) 
There is hereby created in the State Treasury 
the Federal Block Grant Fund, to which there is 
allocated one hundred thirty-five thousand dollars 
($135,000) from those federal block grant funds 
designated by the Budget Act of 1982 for block grant 
administrative costs incurred by administering state 
agencies. 
(b) 
The appropriation by the Budget Act of 1982 
to each administering state agency for federal block 
grant administrative costs shall be reduced by a 
proportionate share for allocation to the Federal Block 


James C. Gross, Esq. 
March 4, 1983  
Grant Fund pursuant to subdivision (a), with the 
exception of the Education and Community Development 
block grants. 
(c) 
The sum of one hundred thirty-five thousand 
dollars ($135,000) is hereby appropriated from the 
Federal Block Grant Fund to the Controller for 
disbursement in support of the olock grant advisory 
task force under Section 16367 during the 1982-83 
fiscal year. 
It is the intent of the Legislature that there 
exist an operating expense budget to cover the 
operational costs of the block grant advisory task 
force required pursuant to Article 1.7 (commencing with 
Section 16366.1). 
These costs shall include, but are 
not limited to, staffing, operating expenses, per diem, 
public notice, and printing costs. 
(d) 
This section shall remain in effect only 
until July 1, 1984, and as of that date is repealed. 
A careful reading of the statutory provision makes it clear 
that these are funds which flow through tne State Treasury. 
The 
sum of $135,000 is appropriated by the Legislature and an 
expense budget is established. 
The fact that tne appropriation 
"sunsets" after one and one-half years does not alter the nature 
of the funding. 
It is always the case that when a new state 
agency or program will become operative on January first~/, 
the legislation creating it must contain an appropriation 
because the agency will not be included in the Budget Act for 
the current fiscal year. 
This does not mean that such an agency 
is not a "state agency" within the meaning of the Act. 
Further-
more, as specifically set forth in Section 16367.2(b) I 
the funds 
here are being reallocated from appropriations contained in the 
Budget Act of 1982 and, consequently, Chap_ 1343 acts as an 
amendment to the Budget Act of 1982.
